input_dict.agent_role: Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.

task_context.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
task_context.agent_role: Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.

left_state.output:
Evidence found:
- Peter Frampton — [4] — 'gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! "'
- "Show Me the Way" — [4] — 'reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100'
- 'originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton"' — [4]
- Rod Stewart — [6] — 'for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".'
- "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" — [6] — 'The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.'
- 'spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977,' — [6]

Ambiguities:
- Which artist/album is meant by "album which reflects on his depressive state" — no passage mentions that phrase.
- Multiple 1976 Hot 100 candidates in the passages: Peter Frampton (passage [4]) vs Rod Stewart (passage [6]).
- Frampton album ambiguity: passage [4] mentions both the 1975 studio album "Frampton" and the 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! " (both referenced for "Show Me the Way").

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing identification of the album (title) and artist described as "reflects on his depressive state".
- Missing confirmation that that specific album (title/artist) had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Frampton Comes Alive!" (album) — to confirm album details and charting songs.
- Page: "Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)" — to confirm chart dates and album attribution.
- Page: "A Night on the Town (Rod Stewart album)" — to confirm album context and singles.
- Page: "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" (Rod Stewart song) — to confirm charting in 1976.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
left_state.hop2_query: depressive album Peter Frampton Rod Stewart Billboard 1976
hop2_titles: Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
The Best of Rod Stewart (1976 album)
hop2_doc_snippets: Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

The Best of Rod Stewart (1976 album) | The Best of Rod Stewart is a compilation album released by Rod Stewart in 1976 (see 1976 in music).  It was released by Mercury Records to complete Rod’s contract before switching labels to Warner Bros. and moving to The United States.  It includes tracks from all his Mercury albums and some non-album singles from the same time period.

Frampton's Camel | Frampton's Camel is Peter Frampton's second album, recorded and released in 1973.  It was the first album that Frampton recorded in the United States.  Most of the album was written in New York City.  It reached number 110 on the "Billboard" 200 Album Chart.

Frampton Comes Alive! II | Frampton Comes Alive!  II is a 1995 live album by English musician Peter Frampton.  It is a sequel to his 1976 multiplatinum album "Frampton Comes Alive".  The title is styled as Frampton Comes Alive II on packaging.

Frampton (album) | Frampton is the fourth studio album by Peter Frampton, released in 1975 before he went on tour and recorded "Frampton Comes Alive! " The most recognizable songs from the album are "Show Me the Way" and "Baby, I Love Your Way" which became big hits when released as singles from "Frampton Comes Alive!"  The album peaked at #32 on the "Billboard" 200.

Frampton Comes Alive! | Frampton Comes Alive!  is a double live album by English rock musician Peter Frampton released in 1976.  It is one of the best-selling live albums in the United States, and considered by many to be one of the finest live rock recordings of all time.  Following four solo albums with little commercial success, "Frampton Comes Alive!"  was a breakthrough for the artist.

Sing It Again Rod | Sing It Again Rod is Rod Stewart's fifth album and first compilation album, released in 1973.  The album is notable for its Peter Corriston-designed die-cut album sleeve (shaped like a glass of whisky, and through which a smiling Stewart can be seen).
left_state.summary_2: Peter Frampton's 1976 live album Frampton Comes Alive! included the song "Show Me the Way", which reached number 6 on the Billboard Hot 100. No supporting evidence in the provided passages states that an album "reflects on his depressive state."

right_state.output:
Evidence found:
- Peter Frampton: "Show Me the Way" reached #6 on the Billboard Hot 100 and is associated with his 1976 live album Frampton Comes Alive! (originally on the 1975 studio album Frampton). (source: "Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)")
- Rod Stewart: "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" was recorded for his 1976 album A Night on the Town and was a US #1 on the Billboard Hot 100, spending 8 weeks at #1 beginning November 13, 1976. (source: "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)")

Ambiguity / missing bridge fact:
- The phrase "album which reflects on his depressive state" does not appear in the retrieved passages. The specific album (title) and artist described as reflecting on a depressive state is not identified in these first-hop documents.
- It is not confirmed whether that particular album (the one described as reflecting on his depressive state) is the same as either of the albums linked above or whether it had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.

Bridge entity needed for next hop:
- Identify the album title and artist described as "reflecting on his depressive state," and then verify whether that album included a song that charted on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976 (and which song and chart date).

Retrieval direction (compact):
- Fetch album and song pages for the 1976 Hot-100 candidates to confirm album-song associations and chart dates: "Frampton Comes Alive!" (album), "Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)", "A Night on the Town (Rod Stewart album)", "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)".
- Retrieve pages that may contain the phrasing about an album reflecting on a depressive state (missing supports / next-hop targets): "Attitudes (band)", "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".

Next step: retrieve the listed targets to (a) identify which album/artist is described as reflecting on his depressive state and (b) confirm whether that album contained a song that charted on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.

hop2_doc_snippets: Attitudes (band) | Attitudes was a rock band from Los Angeles consisting of keyboardist David Foster, guitarist Danny Kortchmar, bass guitarist Paul Stallworth and drummer Jim Keltner.  Its four members came together while working as session musicians during the recording of George Harrison's album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".  One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976.  James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and ...[truncated]

Extra Texture (Read All About It) | Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975.  It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple.  The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album.  The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the  ...[truncated]

Extra! Extra! Read All About It! | Extra!  Extra!  Read All About It!  is an Irish television sitcom that aired on RTÉ 1 for one season in 1993.  The show was written by and starred stand-up comedian Morgan Jones.

You (George Harrison song) | "You" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released as the opening track of his 1975 album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".  It was also the album's lead single, becoming a top 20 hit in America and reaching number 9 in Canada.  A 45-second instrumental portion of the song, titled "A Bit More of You", appears on "Extra Texture" also, opening side two of the original LP format.  Harrison wrote "You" in 1970 as a song for Ronnie Spector, formerly of the R ...[truncated]
Extra Extra | Extra Extra or Extra!  Extra!  Read All About It!  may refer to:

Can't Stop Thinking About You | "Can't Stop Thinking About You" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released in 1975 on his final album for Apple Records, "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".  A love song in the style of a soul/R&B ballad, it was written by Harrison in December 1973, towards the end of his marriage to Pattie Boyd and while he was having an affair with Maureen Starkey, the wife of his former Beatles bandmate Ringo Starr.  Having first considered the song for his 1974 r ...[truncated]

Tired of Midnight Blue | "Tired of Midnight Blue" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released on his 1975 album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".  It was written after a night out with music-industry executives in Los Angeles – an event that Harrison found particularly depressing.  The recording includes contributions from Leon Russell, on piano, and Jim Keltner, who plays drums and percussion.
right_state.summary_2: Extra Texture (Read All About It) is George Harrison's 1975 album whose melancholic mood "reflects Harrison's depressed state." No supporting evidence in the provided passages links that album to Peter Frampton or Rod Stewart or shows it contained a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.

request.rendered_prompt:
You are working with a functional semantic distance over states of one
agent in a multi-agent pipeline.

Each state contains the selected agent's output and may also contain the
downstream consequences produced by that output while the rest of the
pipeline is held fixed. The agent output is the primary object of the
transition. Downstream evidence and metrics are supporting evidence about
its functional effect.

The distance is not surface text edit distance and is not a correctness
score. It measures how much task-relevant behavior and information must
change to move from one state to another.

Judge distance using:
1. Agent-output behavior: what the output is trying to accomplish for the
   selected agent's role.
2. Task-relevant information: which entities, relations, evidence,
   conclusions, or answer constraints are preserved, added, removed, or
   corrected.
3. Downstream consequence: how the output changes retrieval, evidence,
   answerability, or information available to later agents.
4. Transition utility: whether a state is a meaningful intermediate step
   between two endpoint states.

Do not infer distance from text length, token count, document count, or
lexical overlap. Do not treat a state as closer merely because its metric
or final answer is better. Metrics are evidence of functional consequence,
not the distance definition itself.

A midpoint output o_mid between o_left and o_right must:
- preserve useful task-relevant behavior or information from o_left;
- make concrete semantic progress toward o_right;
- differ meaningfully from both endpoint outputs;
- not merely paraphrase or copy o_left;
- not simply copy o_right;
- represent two smaller functional transitions than the original edge.

Generate one midpoint output between the left and right projected states.
The midpoint output will be used directly as the destination output for a
local prompt-update inference step. Do not output a prompt update.

Make the smallest meaningful functional change from the left output toward
the right output. Adopt at least one task-relevant entity, relation,
evidence requirement, conclusion, output property, or behavioral intention
that is present in the right state but weak or absent in the left state.

Required output representation:
Return `mid_output` as one non-empty string containing a summary1 output.

  "mid_output": "Evidence found:
- Peter Frampton: "Show Me the Way" reached #6 on the Billboard Hot 100 and is associated with his 1976 live album Frampton Comes Alive! (originally on the 1975 studio album Frampton).
- Rod Stewart: "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" was a US #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 in late 1976 (multiple weeks at #1) and is linked to his 1976 era album releases.

- A retrieved passage names George Harrison's 1975 album Extra Texture (Read All About It) as having a melancholic mood that "reflects Harrison's depressed state." It is not yet confirmed whether this is the album referenced in the question or whether that album (or any album described as reflecting a depressive state) contained a song that charted on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.

Retrieval direction (next hop):
- Fetch the album page Extra Texture (Read All About It) and its singles (e.g., "You", "Can't Stop Thinking About You", "Tired of Midnight Blue") to verify the "depressive" description and any charting singles.
- Re-check "Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)" and "Frampton Comes Alive!" to confirm release/chart dates.
- Re-check "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" and Rod Stewart album pages to confirm chart dates and album association.
- If Extra Texture is the depressive album, verify whether any track from it (or associated singles) charted on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976; otherwise, continue searching for other albums explicitly described as reflecting an artist's depressive state.",
  "rationale": "This midpoint preserves the first-hop chart evidence for Peter Frampton and Rod Stewart from the left state while adopting the right state's new candidate bridge entity (George Harrison's Extra Texture) that explicitly matches the phrase about an album reflecting a depressive state. It advances the task by naming Extra Texture as the next-hop target and specifying which singles/pages to retrieve for chart confirmation, but it stops short of the right endpoint's additional assertions (e.g., specific 1976 Hot-100 entries linked to that album). Thus it is a meaningful, smaller step between the left (only Frampton/Rod ambiguity) and the right (identifies Harrison album and next retrievals) without copying either endpoint exactly."
}
